claims.claim: Malaysia's Federal Court resumes sittings in May 2026 / this month.
claims.source: No primary Federal Court sitting calendar or cause-list confirmation. Related: Malaysian Bar Circular No. 131/2026 'Court Vacation 2026 | Federal Court of Malaysia (1 to 12 June 2026)', 21 Apr 2026.
claims.correction: Do not state that the Federal Court 'resumes sittings' in May 2026 unless supported by the official judicial calendar or cause list.

claims.claim: The new Chief Justice is Wan Ahmad Farid Wan Salleh.
claims.source: Office of the Chief Registrar, Federal Court of Malaysia, accessed 11 May 2026, https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/en/about-us/court/federal-court/chief-justice
Office of the Chief Registrar, OLY 2026 speech, 12 Jan 2026.
claims.correction: Use exact: 1,522,005 in 2021 to 2,486,567 by November 2025, an increase of 63.37%.

claims.claim: The backlog has grown 63% in four years.
claims.correction: The official statistic is REGISTRATIONS not backlog. Correct wording: 'Criminal case registrations rose 63.37% between 2021 and November 2025.' Do not equate registrations with pending backlog without a separate pending-case statistic.

claims.claim: Two recent apex-court rulings went against rights-claimants in February and April 2026.
claims.source: Federal Court press summary 6 Feb 2026; BERNAMA 20 Apr 2026.
claims.correction: The April 2026 leave-denial went against the applicants. The February 2026 Heidy Quah result was MIXED: the Federal Court reinstated the impugned words in Section 233 but affirmed that there was no basis to prosecute Quah for her post.

claims.claim: Heidy Quah won her free-speech case at the Court of Appeal in 2025.
The Federal Court reinstated the law she had challenged in February 2026.
claims.correction: Narrow: the Federal Court reinstated the impugned WORDS 'offensive' and 'annoy' in Section 233(1)(a) — not the entire law.

claims.claim: The words 'offensive' and 'annoy' in CMA Section 233 — used against critics for years.
claims.correction: Remove or qualify unless supported by charge data, prosecution records, or a rights-report dataset traceable to primary records.

claims.claim: In April 2026, the Federal Court denied seven prisoners leave to challenge their indefinite detention.
claims.correction: For precision: applications under Rule 137 of the Rules of the Federal Court 1995.

claims.claim: They had been imprisoned since childhood.
claims.correction: Sources support that the offences were committed below 18 or during teenage years — not necessarily continuous detention since childhood. Safer: 'They were convicted for offences committed while they were below 18 and had spent many years in detention.'

claims.claim: Detained at 'Ruler's pleasure' under Section 97(2) Child Act.
claims.source: SUHAKAM OCC Media Statement No. 21/2026, 24 Apr 2026.

claims.correction: Verified current age range from BERNAMA is 27 to 36, NOT 30 to 35. Applicants were below 18 at the time the offences were committed. The exact '14-17 when sentenced' range was not verified.

claims.claim: The court said only a royal pardon can release them.
claims.correction: The court identified multiple alternative remedies: Section 97(4) of the Child Act, Regulations 54 and 113 of the Prison Regulations 2000, Article 42 pardon powers, AND yearly Board of Visiting Justices recommendations.

claims.claim: The Chief Justice told judges to deliver on time or take 'a dignified exit.'
In April he cancelled their recess.
claims.source: Malay Mail 27 Apr 2026; Malaysian Bar Circular 104/2026.
claims.correction: The recess or vacation was NOT cancelled. The reported directive was that judges remain on regular duties unless on approved leave, while urgent matters continue during the scheduled vacation. The 1-12 June 2026 vacation still appears in Malaysian Bar circulars.

claims.claim: Court vacation is 'not a holiday,' he said.
claims.source: The Star 27 Apr 2026; Malay Mail 27 Apr 2026.

claims.claim: One Johor Bahru High Court judge handles roughly 800 cases — three times the normal load.
claims.correction: Source: ~800 vs normal 200-250, so about 3.2 to 4 times normal — 'three times' is directionally correct but slightly conservative.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The backlog has grown 63% in four years
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The cited official statistic is for criminal case REGISTRATIONS, not pending backlog. It is also system-wide, not Federal Court-only.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Malaysia's Federal Court resumes sittings this month. The backlog has grown 63% in four years
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The sentence fuses an unverified May 2026 Federal Court sittings claim with a verified system-wide filing increase, creating the impression of a Federal Court backlog statistic that the source does not provide.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Two recent apex-court rulings went against rights-claimants in February and April 2026
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The April case went against the applicants. The Heidy Quah case was MIXED: the constitutional declaration was reversed, but the Federal Court affirmed that her post could not form the basis of prosecution.
The verified reporting says the applicants are now aged 27 to 36; the exact 14-17 sentencing range was not verified.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The court said only a royal pardon can release them
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The court reporting identifies several alternative remedies: Section 97(4), Regulations 54 and 113 of the Prison Regulations 2000, Article 42 pardon powers, AND Board of Visiting Justices recommendations.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: In April he cancelled their recess
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Available sources indicate the vacation remained scheduled, with judges required to remain on duty unless on approved leave.
The broader claim was not verified against primary enforcement records, prosecution data, or official statistics.

omitted_facts: Civil case registrations also rose 59.54% from 303,335 (2021) to 483,933 (Nov 2025) per OLY 2026 speech.
Judicial-capacity context: Superior Courts had 147 judges (14 FC, 27 CoA, 68 HC, 38 Judicial Commissioners) per OLY 2026 speech.
Federal Court Heidy Quah press summary states the full judgment is the only authoritative document.

SUHAKAM Children's Commissioner stated 24 Apr 2026 that Section 97(4)'s annual Board of Visiting Justices review lacks transparency and does not provide sufficient legal certainty for detained children.
BERNAMA reported the seven applicants now aged 27-36, not 30-35.
Court-recess materials confirm scheduled 1-12 June 2026 vacation with urgent-duty arrangements, not a full cancellation.
source_assessment: The strongest source is the official judiciary OLY 2026 speech PDF. The Heidy Quah verification relies on a Federal Court press summary hosted by ARTICLE 19, plus BERNAMA and legal-reporting sources; reasonably strong but still short of the full official judgment. The Ruler's-pleasure case is supported by BERNAMA, Malaysian Bar Circular 150/2026, and SUHAKAM's official statement. Weakest areas: the Federal Court May 2026 sitting/resumption claim, the historical 'used against critics for years' claim, and exact ages of the seven prisoners.
overall_assessment: The analysis has a solid factual core but contains several high-impact overclaims. The verified foundation is solid (63.37% growth, CJ's public warnings, FC restored Section 233 words while preserving Quah's individual protection, seven Ruler's-pleasure detainees failed to obtain leave). However, the article repeatedly converts filings into backlog, implies Federal Court-specific pressure without a Federal Court pending-case statistic, overstates the April recess directive as a cancellation, and gives incorrect or unverified details about the seven prisoners' ages and release routes. It is usable only after tightening the backlog language, correcting the Ruler's-pleasure details, and clearly distinguishing verified court outcomes from editorial framing.
